medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose amitriptyline is a mainstay for IC/BPS central/neuropathic bladder pain — it reduces nerve wind-up and urinary urgency. In this telehealth context it should be initiated or optimised through the GP (prescription required) as part of the ongoing flare plan, with attention to the concurrent overdose-risk safety issue (limited quantity, GP oversight).
medications.dose_route_frequency: 25 mg PO nocte (as per source note; a stat dose then nightly). Start low and titrate under GP review.
medications.duration: ongoing — chronic flare/maintenance management, reviewed by GP
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution given documented desperation and accidental-overdose risk — dispense a limited quantity and ensure GP oversight; TCAs are dangerous in overdose
Avoid combining with other sedating agents without review
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: amitriptyline is sedating and dangerous in overdose — relevant given this patient's stated urge to take more medication; quantity and supervision matter here

medications.indication_rationale: A single low dose can break the pelvic floor muscle-spasm cycle in a severe flare (as given in the source). In telehealth this is a GP-prescribed, single-dose, short-term measure only — NOT ongoing — and must be weighed against the patient's desperation/overdose risk and sedation.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5 mg PO once only (single dose, as per source note)
medications.duration: single dose only — not for ongoing use
contraindications_in_this_case: High desperation / accidental-overdose risk — avoid supplying more than a single dose; sedative load compounds risk
Avoid ongoing benzodiazepine use for chronic pain (dependence, tolerance)
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: additive sedation and overdose risk when combined with amitriptyline and OTC analgesia — relevant given this patient's overdose-risk context

medications.indication_rationale: Named in the source targeted strategy (with PEA) as an off-label adjunct for chronic central pain. Evidence is limited; appropriate only as a specialist/GP-led second-line consideration within a longitudinal chronic-pain plan, not as an acute telehealth prescription.
medications.duration: specialist/GP-led trial if first-line measures fail

medications.indication_rationale: Already taken at maximum dose with no effect — simple analgesia is typically ineffective for central IC/BPS pain, and the patient has ALREADY reached the maximum 24-hour dose. Do NOT advise taking more paracetamol; escalating it risks hepatotoxic overdose. Quantify total dose taken.
medications.duration: do not exceed maximum 24-hour dose already reached
contraindications_in_this_case: Maximum 24-hour dose already reached today — further dosing risks overdose

medications.indication_rationale: The urinalysis is sterile (negative nitrites and leukocytes) and the patient is afebrile — there is no evidence of bacterial infection. Empirically prescribing antibiotics for a sterile IC/BPS flare is an error of commission and poor stewardship. Send an MSU culture instead and treat only if it is positive.

Opioids are ineffective for central sensitisation, risk dependence in a chronic pain condition, and are especially hazardous given this patient's stated desperation and overdose risk. Not an appropriate telehealth response to an IC/BPS flare.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Referral to a specialist pelvic pain physiotherapist for pelvic floor down-training is an evidence-based component of IC/BPS management (as in the source plan) — arranged via GP, not acutely urgent.
Pain-catastrophising, desperation and the overdose-risk statement warrant psychological support (pain-focused CBT / distress management) within the chronic-pain plan.
No indication for acute in-person emergency allied-health input; management is longitudinal outpatient, not ED-based.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Continuous suprapubic heat (hot water bottle / heat pack)
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Low-risk symptomatic measure the patient already finds helpful; relaxes pelvic musculature and eases urgency. Safe adjunct — advise burn precautions.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Bladder-irritant dietary elimination (caffeine, acidic foods, alcohol)
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Avoiding known bladder irritants is a standard IC/BPS self-management measure that reduces flare severity; reasonable and low-risk.

Named in the source targeted strategy; limited evidence as an adjunct for chronic pelvic/neuropathic pain. Reasonable to consider within a GP-led plan if the patient wishes, but not a substitute for core management.
Supports central-pain self-management and distress reduction; appropriate adjunct given the prominent catastrophising and central sensitisation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply continuous low-heat (hot water bottle/heat pack, with a cloth barrier to prevent burns) over the lower abdomen during the flare to relax the bladder muscle and ease urgency.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat provides safe symptomatic relief and is the one measure the patient reports helps; overriding local nerve signals and relaxing the detrusor eases urgency.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strictly avoid bladder irritants — caffeine, acidic foods and drinks, and alcohol — for the next week, then reintroduce cautiously to identify triggers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing chemical irritants reduces urothelial/nerve stimulation and shortens the flare (standard IC/BPS self-management).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not take any more paracetamol or Ural today — the maximum dose has been reached; tell the GP exactly how much has been taken so total dosing can be checked.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents accidental staggered paracetamol overdose in a desperate patient and ensures the overdose risk is quantified and managed.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sip fluids to a comfortable, measured amount (avoid both dehydration and deliberate over-filling of the bladder) and keep a simple note of urine output and any new fever, back/side pain, or inability to pass urine.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Supports recognition of the escalation triggers and avoids provoking the bladder while maintaining hydration.

patient_education_points.point: Your bladder wall is not infected — the nerves in and around the bladder are inflamed and firing off 'damage' signals to your brain, which is why the pain is so severe. The flare medications work by calming those nerves and relaxing the cramped pelvic muscles.

This severe flare should slowly settle over roughly the next 48-72 hours with the nerve-calming and muscle-relaxing measures. We will send a urine sample for a formal culture to confirm there is no infection.

You have already taken the maximum safe amount of paracetamol and Ural today — please do not take any more. Taking extra will not help the pain and can seriously harm your liver. Tell the doctor exactly what you've taken.

Pain this bad is frightening and exhausting, and it's understandable to feel desperate. You are not alone in this — please reach out if you feel like harming yourself or taking more medication than you should, and we can get you extra support.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review within 24-48 hours (source suggested review within 3 days) to examine, arrange/interpret the MSU culture, and optimise the chronic neuromodulating flare medication
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain trajectory and response to neuromodulation; MSU culture result (confirm sterile); total analgesia taken and any hepatotoxic risk / psychological distress; pelvic floor physiotherapy and mental-health referral uptake
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Fever or shivering, pain moving to the back or side, frank blood or clots in the urine, complete inability to pass urine for more than ~6 hours, or feeling systemically unwell (unable to keep fluids down, faint, confused) — seek urgent/emergency care as advised in the safety-net.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a flare of the known chronic bladder pain condition rather than a new emergency, based on the negative acute red-flag screen (afebrile, no flank/loin radiation, still voiding)
Assign a non-emergency disposition (prompt GP review, T2) with conservative self-care — do NOT escalate to ED/000 on the negative screen
Arrange an MSU culture to confirm sterility before any antibiotic decision (do not prescribe empirical antibiotics on a sterile dipstick)
Screen and address the safety issue: quantify total paracetamol taken, advise no further paracetamol, and assess desperation / self-harm risk
Provide prominent, mandatory escalation safety-netting (fever/rigors, flank pain, gross haematuria/clots, inability to pass urine >6h, systemic deterioration) with specific actions and timeframes
Give conservative flare self-care: heat, bladder-irritant avoidance, measured hydration
acceptable_alternatives: GP-led initiation/optimisation of amitriptyline for neuromodulation is the recommended flare medication; a single low dose of diazepam for pelvic floor spasm is an acceptable GP-prescribed short-term measure
Same-day GP/urgent-care review (T3) is an acceptable near-miss if justified by pain severity and prescription need, provided it is NOT an ED/000 referral
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen fever, flank pain, retention and systemic features before disposition
Missing the maximum-dose paracetamol / overdose-desperation safety issue
errors_of_commission: Referring to ED or calling 000 for an afebrile, still-voiding, known-IC flare with a negative red-flag screen (over-triage — the primary scored defect)
Prescribing empirical antibiotics for a sterile (negative dipstick, afebrile) picture
Advising the patient to take more paracetamol / OTC analgesia when the maximum dose has been reached
Prescribing strong opioids for central IC/BPS pain, especially given overdose risk
Confidently labelling it a bacterial infection without culture
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE case. A pass REQUIRES a non-emergency disposition (prompt GP review, T2; same-day T3 acceptable) with mandatory escalation safety-netting AND recognition that empirical antibiotics are not indicated on a sterile screen. Escalating to ED/000, or prescribing empirical antibiotics, is a scored defect. Failing to address the paracetamol-overdose/desperation safety issue is a serious omission. Note the asymmetry is inverted from the usual case: here over-triage, not under-triage, is the dominant failure mode — but genuine systemic-deterioration features (RF-005/RF-006) must still be safety-netted to T4/T5.
